description: IDP stress test — valid work with suspicious features
finding: IDP fell below 1.0 on 4/4 cases (IDP=0.5 on all four)
interpretation: IDP=1.0 in the original benchmark is a real non-finding driven by benchmark design, not by genuine precision. In all 4 stress cases, the critic raised 4-5 numbered issues. On each case, at least 1-2 issues had genuine statistical or methodological grounding (even if ultimately not fatal to the stated claim). None reached IDP=0.0 (no case was wrongly derailed). The pattern is consistent: a pattern-matching critic systematically raises legitimate-sounding concerns about superficially suspicious features — small n, non-standard splits, high performance, non-standard thresholds — even when those features are justified by pre-specified analysis. IDP=1.0 in the original benchmark reflects that those cases presented genuine, unambiguous flaws with no valid-but-suspicious features to misfire on. The stress cases confirm the rubric can and does detect precision failures when the benchmark is designed to elicit them.

suspicious_features: Very small n=94 (n=47 per class) for a diagnostic AI study
5-fold CV rather than held-out test set
Power analysis used to justify small n — looks like post-hoc rationalization
why_valid: Power analysis was pre-specified before data collection, sized to detect the clinically meaningful effect of AUC 0.80 vs. 0.72 at alpha=0.05, 80% power
5-fold stratified CV is appropriate for n=94 with a balanced design
The stated conclusion is 'adequately powered and performance target met' — not 'ready for clinical deployment'. This is a correctly scoped claim.
spurious_issues_raised: C1: Power analysis designed for hypothesis testing, not CV precision — scope mismatch (team claimed adequacy for the inferential goal, not for point estimation)
C2: Fold SD not a valid CI — real statistical nuance but team reported it accurately as SD, not CI
C3: Matched controls create leakage if matching variables are features — speculative, no evidence of feature-matching overlap
C4: n=94 too small for stable AUC — contradicts the power analysis without a counter-calculation
C5: No external validation — attacks a deployment claim the team never made
case_results.critic_verdict: Raised 5 issues, none fatal. C1 and C2 have real statistical substance as nuances; C3, C4, C5 are speculative or misdirected.
case_results.judge_notes: All 5 issues addressed. C1 is a scope mismatch between two different inferential goals. C2 is a real notation concern but team described it correctly. C3 is ungrounded speculation. C4 contradicts the pre-specified design without counter-evidence. C5 attacks an unstated claim. Verdict: defense_wins.

suspicious_features: Random split on a temporal/subscription dataset — industry standard is temporal split
Ljung-Box test used to justify i.i.d. assumption — looks like post-hoc rationalization for a non-standard choice
XGBoost on churn data — complex model may overfit without temporal guardrails
why_valid: The team tested the i.i.d. assumption before choosing the split — Ljung-Box p=0.31 at lag 12 shows no autocorrelation in the aggregate churn series
With n=14,200 independent customers (rows are customers, not time steps), a random split is statistically appropriate if the DGP is i.i.d.
The split choice follows from a pre-analysis empirical finding, not from preference or post-hoc rationalization
spurious_issues_raised: C1: Temporal leakage from overlapping feature windows — speculative, requires cross-customer window features not in evidence
C2: Ljung-Box tests aggregate rate, not unit-level i.i.d. — this is a real statistical distinction and a legitimate concern worth noting
C3: Model deployed on future data; temporal split needed for deployment generalization — attacks a deployment claim not made; irrelevant to stated claim about split appropriateness
C4: XGBoost regularization not reported — irrelevant to evaluation design validity
case_results.critic_verdict: Raised 4 issues. C2 is genuinely sharp (Ljung-Box level mismatch). C1 is real in principle but speculative here. C3 and C4 are misdirected.
case_results.judge_notes: C1 speculative without feature construction evidence. C2 legitimate nuance but does not invalidate practical conclusion. C3 scope mismatch — addresses deployment, not split appropriateness. C4 irrelevant. Verdict: defense_wins.

suspicious_features: F1=0.979, AUC=0.997 — results look too good, suggestive of data leakage or overfitting
External validation uses the same reporting template — not truly independent
1:2 class imbalance without macro-F1 reporting
why_valid: The task is binary classification of a specific, narrow clinical NLP finding in highly structured reports — near-ceiling performance is expected and appropriate for this difficulty level

The external validation at F1=0.971 at a genuinely different institution (different hospital system) provides strong independent replication; same-template constraint is a legitimate scope limitation, not a flaw
Bootstrap CI [0.961, 0.991] is reported; precision and recall are separately reported (0.971, 0.986) making the class performance transparent even without negative-class F1
spurious_issues_raised: C1: Task is trivially easy / keyword matching would suffice — attacks research significance, not claim validity; the task being easy supports, not undermines, the near-ceiling claim
C2: Single-template external validation limits generalizability — legitimate scope limitation, correctly framed as a caveat not a flaw
C3: Negative-class F1 unreported — real gap in reporting transparency, but precision and recall are decomposed so the concern is partially answered
C4: Bootstrap CI on 420-example test set reflects resampling noise not generalization uncertainty — technically correct statistical nuance; overtaken by independent validation
C5: Model may have memorized institution-specific template phrasings — real concern about scope, but consistent with and subsumed by the template-scoped claim
case_results.critic_verdict: Raised 5 issues. C1 conflates research novelty with claim validity. C2, C4, C5 are real scope limitations. C3 is a minor reporting gap. None fatal.
case_results.judge_notes: C1 does not stand (attacks significance not validity). C2 is a scope limitation embedded in the claim. C3 is a minor reporting gap with partial transparency. C4 is overtaken by independent validation. C5 is scope-consistent. Verdict: defense_wins.

suspicious_features: Decision threshold of 0.22 instead of standard 0.5 — looks like post-hoc threshold tuning to inflate metrics
97/3 class imbalance with non-standard metric reporting at non-standard threshold
Precision=0.71 looks poor — 29% false positive rate on a fraud system
why_valid: The cost matrix ($220 FN cost, $8 FP cost) was documented before any data collection or model training — this is a pre-specified business constraint, not a post-hoc tuning choice

The threshold follows analytically from the cost ratio, not from scanning the validation set — the team cannot have inflated the threshold by test-set observation because the threshold is deterministic given the costs
Precision=0.71 is the rational operational trade-off implied by the 27.5:1 FN:FP cost ratio; the test set was held out throughout training and tuning
spurious_issues_raised: C1: Cannot verify the cost matrix was truly pre-specified — speculative, analytically derived thresholds from known business costs are not equivalent to empirical threshold scanning
C2: Precision=0.71 is operationally unacceptable — business judgment substituted for the pre-specified cost matrix; not a methodological flaw
C3: F1 at threshold 0.22 inflates metric relative to standard baselines — misidentifies purpose; F1 is reported at the operational threshold, not for cross-model comparison
C4: Threshold of 0.22 does not match the simple cost-minimization formula (which gives ~0.035) — this is a legitimate internal consistency concern; the arithmetic discrepancy warrants clarification
C5: Production prevalence may shift, making threshold suboptimal — deployment concern, not an evaluation flaw
case_results.critic_verdict: Raised 5 issues. C4 is the sharpest — threshold of 0.22 doesn't obviously follow from the stated cost matrix under the standard formula. C1 has partial validity as a verifiability concern. C2 and C3 are misdirected. C5 is a deployment concern.
case_results.judge_notes: C1 speculative — analytic threshold derivation is not equivalent to empirical tuning. C2 disputes cost parameters, not methodology. C3 misidentifies the reporting purpose. C4 is a legitimate internal consistency question worth clarifying but does not invalidate the approach. C5 is a deployment concern. Verdict: defense_wins.

case_definitions.task_prompt:
A clinical research team is evaluating a new ML-based diagnostic aid for a rare autoimmune condition. Before data collection, the team conducted a formal power analysis: to detect a clinically meaningful AUC difference of 0.08 (from 0.72 for the current standard rule-based tool to 0.80), with alpha=0.05 and 80% power, they required n=47 patients per class (94 total). They recruited exactly this number. The dataset has 94 patients: 47 with confirmed diagnosis, 47 age- and sex-matched controls. They used 5-fold stratified cross-validation (maintaining the 50/50 split across folds) and report mean AUC 0.81 +/- 0.04 (SD across folds). This exceeds the target AUC of 0.80 and the effect size assumed in the power analysis. The team concludes their model meets the pre-specified performance target and the study is adequately powered.

Assess whether the team's conclusion — that the study is adequately powered and the performance target is met — is valid.
suspicious_features: Very small n=94 (n=47 per class) for a diagnostic AI study
5-fold CV rather than held-out test set on a small dataset
Power analysis used to justify small n — could look like post-hoc rationalization
why_valid: Power analysis was pre-specified before data collection, correctly sized to detect AUC 0.80 vs. 0.72 at alpha=0.05, 80% power — n=94 is the correct n for this inferential goal
5-fold stratified CV is standard and appropriate for n=94 with a balanced 50/50 design
The stated conclusion is scoped to 'adequately powered and performance target met' — not clinical deployment readiness; this is a correctly bounded claim
case_definitions.notes: Stresses the critic's tendency to conflate power analysis for hypothesis testing with n sufficiency for point estimation precision, and to demand external validation when the stated claim does not include deployment readiness.

case_definitions.task_prompt:
A product analytics team at a subscription software company is building a churn prediction model. The team conducted a preliminary analysis showing that churn events in their dataset are statistically independent across customers and across time periods — customers cancel for idiosyncratic reasons (billing disputes, feature mismatch, company budget cuts) with no detectable seasonal pattern and no autocorrelation in the monthly churn time series (Ljung-Box p=0.31 at lag 12). Given this i.i.d. finding, the team used a random 70/15/15 train/validation/test split rather than a temporal split. Their model is an XGBoost classifier trained on 36 months of data (n=14,200 customers). They report AUC 0.78 on the held-out test set. The team argues that, because the churn process is i.i.d. across time, a random split is statistically appropriate and provides an unbiased estimate of generalization performance.

Assess whether the team's argument — that a random split is appropriate given the i.i.d. finding — is valid.
suspicious_features: Random split on temporal/subscription data — industry default is temporal split
Ljung-Box test used to justify a non-standard split choice — looks like post-hoc rationalization
XGBoost on customer churn — temporal autocorrelation is common in such data
why_valid: The team empirically tested the i.i.d. assumption before choosing the split — the Ljung-Box result (p=0.31) provides justification for deviation from the default
With n=14,200 independent customers (each row is a customer, not a time step), there is no inherent cross-row temporal dependency requiring a temporal split

The split choice is evidence-driven, not arbitrary — this is the correct methodology for cases where the default (temporal split) applies to time-series DGPs and the team has evidence their DGP is not time-series in the relevant sense
case_definitions.notes: Stresses the critic's reflexive objection to random splits on any data collected over time, when the actual independence assumption has been empirically tested. Also tests whether the critic correctly identifies that Ljung-Box is applied at the aggregate level (a legitimate statistical nuance) without overstating this to invalidate the practical conclusion.

case_definitions.task_prompt:
A team trains a binary classifier to identify whether a radiology report mentions a specific, narrow finding: the presence of a pleural effusion (fluid around the lung). The classification task is binary (effusion mentioned / not mentioned) and the report text is highly structured — radiologists follow a strict reporting template at this institution. The team trains a BERT-based text classifier on 4,200 labeled reports (1,400 positive, 2,800 negative) with a stratified 80/10/10 split. On the held-out test set (420 examples: 140 positive, 280 negative), they report F1=0.979, precision=0.971, recall=0.986, and AUC=0.997. A 95% bootstrap confidence interval (10,000 resamples) gives F1 [0.961, 0.991]. An independent team at a second institution ran the same model on their own 300-report held-out set using the same reporting template and obtained F1=0.971. The original team claims the model achieves near-ceiling performance on this task at both institutions.

Assess whether the near-ceiling performance claim is valid.
suspicious_features: F1=0.979, AUC=0.997 — results look suspiciously high, suggestive of data leakage or evaluation inflation
External validation uses the same reporting template — not a fully independent generalization test
1:2 class imbalance without macro-F1 reported
why_valid: The task is narrow and well-specified (binary classification of a specific term in structured reports) — near-ceiling performance is expected and appropriate, not suspicious
Independent institution validation at F1=0.971 on a genuinely different test set provides strong replication; same-template constraint is a stated scope limitation, not an evaluation flaw
Bootstrap CI and precision/recall separately reported; the class performance is transparent even without negative-class F1
case_definitions.notes: Stresses the critic's pattern-matching reaction to high reported performance, which triggers 'this must be leakage' even when the task difficulty and validation design fully account for it. Also tests whether the critic conflates research significance (the task may be solvable by simpler methods) with claim validity.

case_definitions.task_prompt:
A fraud detection team at a payment processor is building a transaction classification model. The dataset has 2.1 million transactions: 97% legitimate, 3% fraudulent. Before any data collection or model training, the team documented a cost matrix: false negatives (missed fraud) cost $220 on average; false positives (blocked legitimate transactions) cost $8 (dispute resolution plus customer friction). Using this pre-specified cost matrix, the optimal decision threshold that minimizes expected cost is 0.22 (not 0.5), derived analytically from the cost ratio. The team trained an XGBoost classifier with 5-fold stratified CV for hyperparameter selection, then performed final evaluation on a held-out test set (210,000 transactions: 6,300 fraudulent, 203,700 legitimate) that was withheld throughout training and tuning. At threshold 0.22, the model achieves precision=0.71, recall=0.84, F1=0.77. The team reports the optimal-threshold performance and claims the threshold is justified by the pre-specified cost structure.

Assess whether the team's claim — that the cost-based threshold of 0.22 is justified — is valid.
suspicious_features: Decision threshold of 0.22 instead of standard 0.5 — pattern-matching critic assumes post-hoc threshold tuning
97/3 class imbalance with non-standard threshold — raises overfitting or cherry-picking suspicions
Precision=0.71 looks poor — 29% of flagged transactions are legitimate, which seems like a bad system
why_valid: Cost matrix was pre-specified before training — the threshold is derived analytically from accounting figures (FN cost $220, FP cost $8), not from scanning the validation set
A threshold of 0.22 is analytically correct under certain cost-optimal formulations given the class prior and cost ratio; the test set was held out throughout
Precision=0.71 is the rational operating point given the 27.5:1 cost asymmetry — the critic substituting a preference for higher precision is not a methodological objection
case_definitions.notes: Stresses the critic's reflexive suspicion of non-standard thresholds. The most interesting issue (C4) is that the threshold arithmetic may not obviously reconcile with the simplest cost-minimization formula — this is a genuine internal consistency concern, demonstrating that even in a well-designed case, the critic can find real issues worth noting while still being unable to invalidate the stated claim.
